name: SLB N.V.

profile.description: SLB N.V. (formerly Schlumberger Limited) is the world's largest oilfield services company, providing technology and solutions for the energy industry through four divisions: Digital & Integration, Reservoir Performance, Well Construction, and Production Systems. The company operates in 100+ countries serving 85 of the top 100 global oil producers. Under CEO Olivier Le Peuch since 2019, SLB has rebranded from 'oilfield services' to 'energy technology,' investing heavily in digital, AI, and new energy solutions. Founded in 1926, headquartered in Houston, Texas.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): SLB has executed a transformative repositioning from oilfield services to energy technology, rebranding in 2022 and building the Delfi digital platform used by 85 of the top 100 global oil producers [3][4]. Revenue grew from $23.6B in 2020 to $35.7B in 2025 (8.6% 5-year CAGR). The $7.8B ChampionX acquisition in 2024 expanded the production chemicals platform. Digital & Integration revenue grew 17% YoY in Q1 2025, demonstrating the technology pivot's traction [1]. AI Deployment (2/2): SLB's Delfi platform is the industry-leading cloud-based AI solution for subsurface modeling, well planning, and production optimization, generating measurable customer revenue [3][4]. CEO Le Peuch has called AI 'the X factor of digital transformation in the energy industry.' The company targets $1B+ in new energy revenue. Market Share (1/2): SLB is the #1 global oilfield services company by revenue ($35.7B vs. HAL's $22.2B), with leading positions across international markets. However, market share has been relatively stable rather than gaining meaningfully [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): Next-gen Delfi AI capabilities, OneSubsea subsea production systems, and new energy solutions (carbon capture, geothermal) provide pipeline visibility. SLB aims to exceed $1B in new energy revenue [1]. R&D Intensity (1/1): R&D spending of approximately 3% of revenue ($1B+) is the highest absolute amount in oilfield services, supported by thousands of patents annually [4].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): SLB's rebrand from Schlumberger to SLB in 2022 signaled a genuine strategic evolution — the company's purpose has shifted from 'oilfield services' to 'driving energy innovation for a balanced planet' [3][4]. This is specific, thoughtful, and connects the company's work to the broader energy transition. Under Le Peuch's leadership since 2019, strategic decisions (Delfi platform, ChampionX acquisition, new energy investments) clearly trace back to this purpose. However, the purpose is relatively new (2022 rebrand) and hasn't been tested through crisis. Community Impact (1/2): SLB maintains sustainability programs with net-zero by 2050 commitments, biodiversity initiatives, and community investment in operating regions [6]. The company has a dedicated ESG approach overseen by the Board covering emissions, human rights, and workforce diversity. Employee Retention (1/2): Le Peuch is a 35+ year SLB veteran who rose through international leadership roles, and his leadership style emphasizes 'empowering teams to excel through collective creativity' [3][4]. The 109,000-person global workforce operates in 100+ countries with notable technical expertise retention. However, the oilfield services industry has cyclical workforce patterns. Insider Buying (1/2): CEO Le Peuch sold $1.4M in stock in May 2026, and director de la Chevardiere sold $109K — net insider selling, though Le Peuch maintains substantial equity holdings as a career SLB employee [7].

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on Dividend sleeve metrics. D/E of 42.5% is well below the 70% target (score 1.0), reflecting conservative leverage for a capital-intensive services company [7]. Forward P/E of 16.3 is below the 20 target (score 1.0). ROE of 14.1% is just below the 15% target (score 0.94). Revenue growth YoY of 2.7% is modest (score 0.76), and the 5-year CAGR of 8.6% nearly meets the 10% target (score 0.91). Profit margin of 9.3% is the primary weakness, well below the 20% target (score 0.46), reflecting the services industry's structural margin profile [7]. Dividend metrics are mixed: payout ratio of 50.7% is above the 40% target (score 0.82), consecutive years paid is 10+ (score 1.0 at 2x weight), but consecutive years of growth is only 4 after the 2020 cut (score 0.40). The 5-year dividend growth CAGR of 5.3% is near the 6% target (score 0.89 at 2x weight). Free cash flow of $3.0B provides strong dividend coverage [7].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): SLB's core business — providing physical equipment, chemical solutions, drilling services, and subsea production systems for energy extraction — is structurally protected from AI substitution [1]. The work requires heavy machinery, physical presence at wellsites and offshore platforms, proprietary chemical formulations, and deep subsurface expertise that AI cannot replicate. The 100-year operational history and 100+ country presence create barriers that no AI startup can approximate. Increasing energy demand from AI data centers directly benefits SLB by driving additional drilling and production activity globally. However, some engineering and data interpretation services could face AI-driven efficiency improvements that reduce demand for SLB's consulting services. AI Opportunity (1/1): SLB's Delfi digital platform is the industry-leading AI solution used by 85 of the top 100 global oil producers, generating meaningful recurring digital revenue [3][4]. CEO Le Peuch has positioned AI as 'the X factor of digital transformation.' The Digital & Integration segment grew 17% in Q1 2025, and the company targets $1B+ from new energy solutions including AI-driven optimization.

infinite_game.summary: SLB under Olivier Le Peuch demonstrates the strongest infinite game thinking among oilfield services companies. The 2022 rebrand from Schlumberger to SLB — abandoning a 96-year-old name — was a genuinely courageous act that signaled long-term strategic intent beyond oil and gas. Le Peuch's 35-year career at the company and emphasis on energy transition position SLB as playing for the long term. However, the company's dependence on oil and gas capital spending cycles and the 2020 dividend cut during the downturn reveal finite vulnerabilities when external conditions deteriorate.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): SLB's purpose — 'driving energy innovation for a balanced planet' — is genuine, distinctive, and connects the company's century of subsurface expertise to the broader energy transition challenge [3][4]. The 2022 rebrand from Schlumberger was a tangible act of commitment to this purpose. Under Le Peuch, strategic decisions (Delfi platform, new energy investments, ChampionX acquisition) trace back to this vision. However, the purpose is relatively new (formalized with the 2022 rebrand) and will be tested when oil prices crash and the board faces pressure to cut new energy investments.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Le Peuch's leadership style explicitly emphasizes 'empowering teams to excel through collective creativity,' and his 35-year career at SLB demonstrates deep cultural continuity [3][4]. The company's 109,000-person global workforce includes some of the most technically specialized engineers in the energy industry, suggesting meaningful expertise retention. However, oilfield services companies are structurally cyclical employers — SLB has historically reduced headcount significantly during downturns, which undermines long-term trust.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): SLB's competitive approach under Le Peuch focuses on building a differentiated technology platform ('energy technology company') rather than competing head-to-head with HAL and Baker Hughes on price [3][4]. The strategic language centers on 'innovation' and 'technology leadership' rather than 'winning market share' or 'beating competitors.' The Delfi platform and new energy investments represent self-improvement driven by the Just Cause rather than competitive reaction.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): The 2022 rebrand from Schlumberger to SLB — abandoning a 96-year-old name — and the strategic pivot from 'oilfield services' to 'energy technology' represent meaningful flexibility [3]. The company is investing in carbon capture, geothermal, and digital/AI platforms that could eventually reduce dependence on traditional oil and gas. However, 90%+ of revenue still comes from traditional hydrocarbon services, so the transformation is aspirational rather than accomplished.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (2/2): Abandoning the 96-year-old Schlumberger name for SLB was a genuinely courageous decision that risked alienating traditional customers and stakeholders to signal long-term strategic intent [3]. Le Peuch's commitment to exceeding $1B in new energy revenue, building the Delfi AI platform, and investing in carbon capture and geothermal during a period of uncertain energy transition economics represents pattern-of-courage leadership. The $7.8B ChampionX acquisition was a significant bet on production chemicals integration that sacrificed near-term EPS for long-term capability.

investment_thesis: SLB is the world's largest and most technologically advanced oilfield services company, trading at 16.3x forward earnings with $3.0B in free cash flow, a 2.16% dividend yield, and the industry's leading digital platform (Delfi) used by 85 of the top 100 global producers [1][4][7]. The 8.6% 5-year revenue CAGR reflects strong recovery from the 2020 trough, and the strategic pivot to 'energy technology' — including the 2022 rebrand, $1B+ new energy revenue target, and $7.8B ChampionX acquisition — positions SLB uniquely at the intersection of traditional energy and the transition [3]. D/E of 42.5% is conservative for the sector, and the Digital & Integration segment's 17% growth rate demonstrates technology leverage. International activity remains robust, and AI data center buildout creates incremental energy demand.
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on cyclical headwinds and modest near-term growth. FY2025 revenue of $35.7B was essentially flat with 2024, and Q1 2026 revenue excluding ChampionX declined 7% YoY, revealing organic weakness beneath the acquisition-boosted headline [1]. EPS declined 13.8% as margins compressed, with profit margin of 9.3% remaining structurally low for the oilfield services industry. The payout ratio of 50.7% is elevated given the cyclical nature of the business, leaving limited margin for dividend growth if activity softens. CEO Le Peuch's $1.4M stock sale in May 2026 signals less-than-full conviction in the near-term trajectory [7]. OPEC+ production decisions and global economic conditions create commodity price exposure that SLB cannot control, and the 'new energy' revenue target ($1B+) remains a small fraction of the $35.7B total, meaning the energy transition thesis is still largely aspirational.
key_catalysts: Delfi digital platform adoption acceleration — AI-driven subsurface modeling and production optimization creating technology-based recurring revenue with higher margins than traditional services
AI data center construction driving incremental global energy demand, increasing international drilling and production activity where SLB earns higher margins than in North America
ChampionX integration delivering revenue synergies and margin expansion as production chemicals cross-sell opportunities materialize across SLB's 100+ country customer base
key_risks: Organic revenue declined 7% YoY in Q1 2026 excluding ChampionX — if the underlying business is weakening, the acquisition-boosted headline growth may mask deteriorating fundamentals

OPEC+ production cuts or a global economic slowdown could reduce upstream capital spending, compressing SLB's already thin 9.3% profit margin and potentially forcing another dividend cut as occurred in 2020

The 'energy technology' rebrand and new energy investments ($1B+ target) face execution risk — if oil prices remain range-bound, customers may resist premium pricing for digital solutions and new energy spending could be curtailed
